prompt: What is something small that brought you comfort today?
Who in your life are you quietly thankful for, and why?
Describe a moment from the past week that you want to remember.
What is something you believed a year ago that you no longer do?
Where are you being too hard on yourself?
What would you do differently if you weren't afraid of failing?
What has been weighing on your mind lately, and why?
When did you last feel truly at peace? What were the conditions?
What does your ideal ordinary day look like?
If your current mood were a weather pattern, what would it be?
Describe your life right now as if it were the opening line of a novel.
What color best represents how you feel this week, and why?
What do you wish someone in your life understood about you?
Describe a conversation you keep putting off. What's stopping you?
Who do you show up best for, and what does that reveal about you?
What sensations are you aware of in your body right now?
What thought keeps returning to you today? Just notice it — don't judge it.
What would it feel like to let go of one expectation you're holding?
What was I worried about that turned out not to mater?
Where have I grown in ways I didn't notice while it was happening?
What old questions still feel alive and deserve more time?
What am I carrying that I haven't said out loud to anyone?
What emotion have I been avoiding? What if I turned towards it?
When did I last feel genuinely at peace? What was happening?
What does my inner critic say most often? Is any of it true?
What am I grieving, even queitly or something small?
What would I feel if I stopped trying to fix myself for one day?
When do I feel most like myself? Least like it?
If my emotions today were weather, what is the forecast?
What do I need right now that I am not giving myself?
What is the kindest thing I could say to myself today/right now?
What would I tell a close friend feeling exactly this?
What is one true thing I can say about just this moment?
What has survived in me that I thought wouldn't?
If this difficulty were a teacher, what is it showing me?
What would 'enough' look like today? Not perfect, just enough.
Did today reflect what I say I value?
Where did I take the easy path when I knew there was a harder, more honest one?
What small thing happened today that I want to remember?
What am I trying to build, and who is it actually for?
What would I research or teach if nobody was evaluating it?
Where do my academic interests and my personal beliefs feel in tension?
What does a fair world actually look like in concrete terms, not abstractly but in daily life?
Where do I see my values reflected in the world around me, even in small ways?
What belief have I held for a long time that I'm starting to question?
What specifically am I angry at today? What is the anger telling me?
Where is the line between righteous frustration and consuming resentment?
Who do I find it hardest to extend understanding to and why?
What did I read that stopped me? What was it actually saying?
Where did I notice a gap today between how I want to move through the world and how I actually did?
What would it look like to fully accept this moment without abandoning the desire to change things?
What do I actually believe happens after we dies, not what I'm supposed to believe?
What would I do differently if I had one year left to live?
What does a good life look lide, specifically?
What is the difference between happiness and meaning? Which do I want?
What do I think the purpose of suffering is?
What does freedom mean tom me and am I living it?
What is the relationship between love and fear in how I live?
What would it mean to fully accept that everyything is impermanent?
Is there a gap between who I am and who I want to be?
If I could ask one question and receive a completely honest answer, what would it be?
What do I believe about God, the sacred, or the largest thing I could imagine?
What moment of beauty stopped me this week, even briefly?
When did I last feel genuine wonder and what produced it?
What would I create if I knew no one would ever see it?
What does the natural world tell me that people don't?
What story am I telling about my life? IS it the truest version?
If I wrote a poem right now, even a terrible one, what would it be about?
What has sitting taught me that I couldn't learn any other way?
When my mind wonders, where does it go and what does that tell me?
Where is the cushion leaking into daily life, even in small ways?
What teaching from my reading has stayed with me this week?
What does self-compassion actually feel like when I manage it?
If my practice were a living thing, is it a seedling, sapling, or oak?
What is the most uncomfortable thing practice has shown me about myself?

prompt: What is breaking my hear about the world and what am I doing with that?

prompt: Where do I have more influence than I think I do?

prompt: What kind of ancestor do I want to be to my community?

prompt: Where am I confusing concern with despair? What would transform one into the other?

category: The World and Your Place In It
prompt: How do I want to be changed by everything I am reading and practicing?

The dominant legal interpretation of fiduciary duty holds that boards must maximize shareholder value. But fiduciary duty in other professions — medicine, law, financial advising — means loyalty to the client's genuine interests, not their momentary preferences. What would corporate governance look like if boards were held to the same standard as a physician or attorney?

Milton Friedman argued in 1970 that the social responsibility of business is to increase profits. That argument rested on specific assumptions about markets, information, and externalities. Which of those assumptions no longer hold in 2025 — and what does their failure imply for the doctrine itself?

Delaware corporate law gives boards considerable discretion under the Business Judgment Rule. How might that discretion be reinterpreted to accommodate stakeholder interests without requiring legislative reform? What precedents already exist?

The Zen concept of interbeing — that nothing exists independently of everything else — is philosophically incompatible with the idea that a corporation's obligations end at the shareholder. Write a short essay arguing that interdependence is not merely an ethical preference but an accurate description of how corporations actually function in complex systems.

If a board has a fiduciary duty to act in the long-term interests of the corporation, and short-termism demonstrably destroys long-term value, is short-termism itself a breach of fiduciary duty? Build the argument.

Corporations are legal persons. What would it mean to hold a corporation to the same standard of character we expect of a virtuous person in the Aristotelian sense — and which governance structures would that require?

Aristotle distinguished between techne (technical skill) and phronesis (practical wisdom — knowing what the right thing to do is in a particular situation). Most MBA programs teach techne almost exclusively. What would a curriculum centered on phronesis look like, and what does its absence cost organizations?

Marcus Aurelius governed an empire while practicing Stoic philosophy daily. What is the relationship between a leader's inner life and their outer conduct? Can ethical leadership be sustained without some form of contemplative practice?

The Zen concept of beginner's mind — approaching situations without the accumulated assumptions of expertise — has direct implications for leadership decision-making. Where does expertise become an obstacle to sound judgment, and how might organizations institutionalize the practices that counteract this?

Identify a leader you consider genuinely ethical — from history, your professional experience, or public life. What specific practices, commitments, or structures enabled their ethical conduct? Were those factors personal, institutional, or both — and which is more reproducible?

The Stoic dichotomy of control — distinguishing what is and is not within our power — has direct applications to risk management and ethical decision-making under uncertainty. Where does this framework align with and diverge from conventional risk frameworks in your field?

Hannah Arendt wrote about the banality of evil — ordinary people doing terrible things not out of malice but out of thoughtlessness, role-following, and failure to think. What are the organizational equivalents of this phenomenon, and what structural safeguards might prevent it?

What is the difference between a leader who is ethical because they fear reputational or legal consequences and one who is ethical because of genuine character? Does the distinction matter for outcomes — and how would you measure it?

R. Edward Freeman's stakeholder theory argues that corporations create value for and with stakeholders — employees, customers, communities, suppliers — not just shareholders. Twenty years after it became mainstream in management literature, why has practice changed so little? Is the problem theoretical, structural, incentive-based, or something else?

The Business Roundtable's 2019 Statement on the Purpose of a Corporation explicitly abandoned shareholder primacy in favor of stakeholder commitments. Three years later, most signatories' governance structures remained unchanged. What does this gap between declaration and practice reveal — and what would close it?

Thich Nhat Hanh's concept of interbeing and Freeman's stakeholder theory arrive at structurally similar conclusions from entirely different starting points — one from Buddhist ethics, one from strategic management. Write a comparative analysis of their convergence and divergence. What does each tradition offer that the other lacks?

ESG frameworks have been criticized from both the left (greenwashing, insufficient teeth) and the right (politicization of capital, fiduciary overreach). Steelman both critiques. Where do you land — and what would a more defensible framework look like?

Indigenous governance traditions in many cultures embed obligations to future generations directly into decision-making structures — the Iroquois Confederacy's seventh-generation principle being the most cited example. What would it mean to operationalize a seventh-generation obligation in a publicly traded corporation's governance framework?

If corporations exist within communities and depend on them for labor, infrastructure, customers, and legitimacy, can the externalization of costs onto those communities ever be genuinely ethical — or is it always a form of subsidy extraction? Build the argument either way.

The shareholder primacy doctrine assumes that shareholders bear the residual risk of the enterprise. But in large modern corporations, employees — who cannot diversify their human capital — often bear more concentrated risk than diversified shareholders. Does this alter the ethical calculus of who governance should primarily serve?

Karl Polanyi argued in The Great Transformation that the market economy is not a natural phenomenon but a political construction — and that treating land, labor, and money as pure commodities destroys the social fabric that markets themselves depend upon. What are the contemporary manifestations of this dynamic, and what follows for business ethics?

The doctrine of shareholder primacy emerged from a specific intellectual moment — Chicago School economics in the 1970s — and was institutionalized through specific mechanisms. If it is a historical construction rather than a natural law, what does that imply for reform?

Buddhist economics — articulated by E.F. Schumacher in Small Is Beautiful — proposes that the purpose of work is meaningful contribution and that genuinely good economics would minimize suffering rather than maximize throughput. Where does this framework align with and challenge mainstream economic assumptions? Is it academically defensible or merely aspirational?

Capitalism has produced extraordinary material abundance and extraordinary inequality, environmental destruction, and commodification of human relationships. Rather than defending or condemning it wholesale, identify the specific mechanisms that produce each outcome — and which mechanisms are separable from the system's value-generating core.

The corporation as a legal form was invented to serve specific social purposes — enabling large-scale capital aggregation for projects that required resources beyond any individual. Has the form remained faithful to that purpose — and if not, what reform of the legal form itself would realign it?

Joanna Macy argues that industrial society operates on a chain of production story that renders invisible both its dependence on the natural world and its obligations to future generations. How might making these dependencies visible — through accounting reform, legal standing for nature, or governance restructuring — alter corporate behavior?

Cybersecurity risk is conventionally framed as a technical and financial problem. Reframe it as an ethical problem: when an organization fails to protect the data of customers, employees, or patients, who has been wronged, in what way, and what is owed? What governance structures would follow from taking this framing seriously?

The fiduciary duty argument applies with particular force to data stewardship. Organizations collect vast amounts of personal data and make decisions affecting individuals' lives, opportunities, and safety. What would it mean to hold a board fiduciarily responsible for data stewardship in the same way they are responsible for financial stewardship?

AI systems increasingly make consequential decisions in hiring, lending, medical diagnosis, and criminal justice. These decisions can be opaque, biased, and unappealable. What ethical framework should govern AI deployment in high-stakes contexts, and who within an organization bears responsibility when these systems cause harm?

The Stoic dichotomy of control maps onto risk management in interesting ways. In cybersecurity, practitioners distinguish between risk reduction (what we control) and risk transfer (what we don't). Where does ethical responsibility lie when harm occurs despite reasonable precautions — and does that line shift as technology makes more things controllable?

Technology companies routinely externalize the psychological, social, and political costs of their platforms onto users and society while capturing the financial value. Apply the stakeholder framework: who are the full stakeholders of a social media platform, what does each bear and receive, and what governance structure would make that distribution equitable?

The precautionary principle in environmental law holds that when an action risks harm, the burden of proof falls on those proposing the action to demonstrate it is not harmful. Apply this principle to AI deployment, genetic technology, or cybersecurity risk. What would precautionary governance look like in practice?

You have deep expertise in cybersecurity risk management and GRC, and you are developing a philosophical framework centered on fiduciary duty, stakeholder theory, and contemplative ethics. Where is the specific contribution only you can make — the intersection of practitioner knowledge and scholarly framework that no pure academic or pure practitioner could occupy alone?

The Bodhisattva vow — to delay personal liberation to serve all beings — is structurally similar to a genuine stakeholder conception of fiduciary duty. Write a short essay exploring this parallel: what does each tradition illuminate about the other, and what would a governance theory grounded in both look like?

Virtue ethics, Zen practice, stakeholder theory, and systems thinking all converge on a similar insight: that the self is not isolated, that actions ripple outward, and that genuine flourishing is relational rather than individual. What are the implications of this convergence for how we teach business ethics — not as a compliance function but as a practice of character?

If you were to articulate a unified theory of ethical business governance that integrated your cybersecurity expertise, your philosophical reading, and your emerging contemplative practice, what would its three or four core propositions be? Write them as thesis statements — precise, arguable, and significant.

Most business ethics education focuses on dilemmas — trolley problems in corporate clothing. But Aristotle argued that ethics is primarily about character formation, not dilemma resolution: a person of genuine virtue rarely faces dilemmas because their character has already resolved the question. What would business ethics education look like if it took this seriously?

You are both a practitioner and an emerging scholar. What do you know from professional experience that the academic literature on business ethics gets wrong, underweights, or simply cannot see from the outside? That gap is where your most original contribution lives. Write toward it.
